title: Java Booleans
content:
### Booleans in Java

A boolean type represents a value that is either `true` or `false`.

---

### Boolean Declaration

```java
boolean isJavaFun = true;
boolean isFishTasty = false;
```

---

### Boolean Expressions

Boolean values are often returned by comparison operators:

| Expression | Result |
|------------|--------|
| `10 > 5` | `true` |
| `10 == 5` | `false` |
| `10 <= 5` | `false` |
| `10 != 5` | `true` |

---

### Boolean with Conditional Statements

```java
if (isJavaFun) {
    System.out.println("Java is fun!");
}

int age = 18;
boolean canVote = age >= 18;
if (canVote) {
    System.out.println("You can vote");
}
```

---

### Logical Operators with Booleans

| Expression | Result |
|------------|--------|
| `true && true` | `true` |
| `true && false` | `false` |
| `true || false` | `true` |
| `false || false` | `false` |
| `!true` | `false` |
| `!false` | `true` |

> **Key Point:** Booleans are not numeric in Java. You cannot use `0` or `1` as boolean values. Use boolean expressions directly in conditions without comparing to `true` or `false`.

quiz.question: What values can a boolean have?
